sections.paragraph:
-

(1) to pass over, to pass by, pr.n. תִּפְסַח Thapsacus, where the Euphrates was crossed. Hence-

(2) to pass over, to spare, Isa 31:5 followed by עַל Exo 12:13, 23 Exo 12:23, 27 Exo 12:27, i.q. עָבַר עַל. (Arabic فسح to make more room for any one).

[Not divided into two parts in Thes.].

II. פָּסַח pr. to be wrenched, dislocated (Arab. فسخ), hence to halt. Used figuratively, 1Ki 18:21, “how long halt ye between two opinions?” i.e. fluctuate from one to the other. A similar use is made in Arabic of the verb حنف to halt, to limp, in Syriac of the verb ܚܓܪ (Barhebr. p. 531), and Chrysostom used the phrase χωλεύειν περὶ τὰ δόγματα.

Piel, id. 1Ki 18:26 (used scornfully of the awkward leaping of the priests of Baal).

Niphal, to be made lame, 2Sa 4:4.

Derivatives, פָּסֵחַ-פִּסֵּחַ.
